content.full_text:
State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
: Advice Files 
From 
: 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Judy Davis 
Subject: 83030(a), 84216, 87207 
Date 
: April 15, 1983 
{fM-83-105 
Reporting of loans on campaign statements and SET's 
At the advice request meeting held 4/14/83, the following 
was decided about the attached questions: 
1. 
The only apparent violation under these circumstances is 
the misreporting of the interest rate. 
If the loan to the candi-
date did call for interest at 12%, the committee can repay bath 
principal and interest to the lender, and these repayments will 
not be income to the candidate reportable on his SEI. 
2. 
The committee shows the loan as having been repaid by 
the candidate. 
At the point where the proceeds of the sale are 
applied to payoff the loan, the candidate has made either a con-
tribution or a loan to his committee, which should be reported 
by the committee on either Schedule A or Schedule B, depending on 
whether the candidate expects to be repaid. 
The candidate reports 
on the property schedule of his SEI that the property was disposed 
of on the date of the trustee's sale, and lists the Durchaser at 
the trustee's sale as a source of income to him on the income 
schedule of the SEI. 
3. 
There is no question of personal use when a candidate is 
repaid money he lent to his committee at 0% and then relends the 
committee money at a higher interest rate to cover the cost of 
borrowing the money with which he was repaid. 
There is nothing 
reportable on the SEI except (depending on the ultimate answers 
to the questions presented to the same advice request meeting in 
Barbara Milman's 4/13/83 memo) the loan from the bank. 
4, 
The judgment is an "outstanding debt" and continues to :,e 
reportable even though technically it is not a loan. 
JSD: sf 


State of Colifornlo 
Memorandum 
To 
OPINION REQUEST PARTICIPANTS 
Date 
April 14, 1983 
From 
: 
FAIR POLJ11CAL ~CT1CI5 COMMISSION 
Judy Davis p 
Additional questions regarding loans: 
1. 
The secured loan is to the candidate at 12%; the 
lender knows the proceeds are to be used in the campaign 
and makes the check payable to the committee. 
The 
committee reports the loan on its campaign statement as 
from the lender at 0%. 
(Assume this is a large loan and 
12% interest would amount to more than $20,000). 
Aside from the fact that the committee has misrepresented 
the interest rate on the loan, has the candidate made a 
contribution to his committee for the amount of interest due? 
Can the committee legi timately pay the lender the int.erest 
under these circumstances? 
If it can and does, are the interest 
payments income to the candidate? 
2. 
The secured loan comes due and the committee has a 
deficit and cannot repay. 
The lender files a notice of 
default and the candidate's property securing t~e loan is 
sold at a trustee's sale. 
How should this be reported: 
(i.e. committee writes loan off? 
Purchaser at trustee's 
sale becomes source of income to public official?) 
3. 
A candidate is running for an office with a four 
year term. 
During the election year, he lends his committee 
$70,000 at 0% interest. 
The following year, he obtains an 
unsecured personal loan from a local bank at prime for 
$40,000. 
He puts the loan proceeds into his campaign 
account, reporting the loan as being from the bank. 
The 
committee uses the proceeds to repay $40,000 of the $70,000 
in loans at 0% it owes to the candidate. 
The candidate 
thereupon re-lends the $40,000 to his committee at prime +2%. 


·V 
~" 
1', 
_ 
; 
tb 
/ 
Opinion Request Participants 
April 14, 1983 
f 
Page two 
The loan from the bank requires quarterly payments of 
interest only. 
The committee makes the first quarterly' 
interest payment directly' to the candidate. 
The next three 
payments are made by the committee directly to the bank. 
(During this period of time, the committee is making 
virtually no expenditures other than loan repayments and 
loan interest). 
Are the interest payments made to the bank income to 
the candidate? 
Does it make a difference who the committee 
pays the interest to? 
Is this a vehicle for transmuting 
personal income to a campaign contribution, thereby avoiding 
personal use restrictions? 
Or is it merely legitimate 
restructuring of an outstanding debt? 
4. 
A public official obtains a loan for business 
purposes for over $100,000 from a local private lender. 
At the time the loan is made, the lender believes it to 
be secured by property owned by the public official in the 
jurisdiction. 
However, because the public official has 
fraudently represented the nature of his interest in the 
property securing the loans, the lender is unable to 
satisfy the debt by forcing a sale of the property. 
The 
lender thereupon sues on the note and obtains a judgment 
for the full amount of the loan plus interest. 
Does the definition of income as including "an 
outstanding loan" require reporting of the loan after 
judgment has been obtained? 
(The legal effect of the 
judgment is to cancel the loan indebtedness and replace 
it with indebtedness based on the judgment). 
JD:lh

sections.question_synthetic: How should loans be reported on campaign statements and Statements of Economic Interest (SEI)? Specifically: (1) When a candidate takes a secured loan at 12% interest but the committee reports it at 0%, has the candidate made a contribution for the interest amount, and are interest payments income to the candidate? (2) When a secured campaign loan defaults and the candidate's property is sold at a trustee's sale, how should this be reported? (3) When a candidate lends money to his committee at 0%, is repaid using bank loan proceeds, then re-lends at a higher rate, does this constitute personal use of campaign funds? (4) After a fraudulently secured loan results in a judgment, must the judgment still be reported as an outstanding loan on the SEI?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: (1) The only violation is the misreporting of the interest rate; the committee can repay both principal and interest to the lender, and these repayments are not income to the candidate reportable on the SEI. (2) When the property is sold at a trustee's sale, the candidate has made either a contribution or loan to the committee (reportable on Schedule A or B), must report the property disposal on the SEI property schedule, and must list the purchaser at the trustee's sale as an income source on the SEI. (3) There is no personal use issue when a candidate is repaid a 0% loan and re-lends at a higher rate to cover borrowing costs; the bank loan may need SEI reporting. (4) A judgment replacing a loan is an "outstanding debt" that continues to be reportable on the SEI even though technically it is no longer a loan.
sections.parsing_notes: Internal FPPC memo format; no standard Q/C/F/A sections. Synthetic Q&C manually drafted from readable text.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: How should loans be reported on campaign statements and Statements of Economic Interest (SEI)? Specifically: (1) When a candidate takes a secured loan at 12% interest but the committee reports it at 0%, has the candidate made a contribution for the interest amount, and are interest payments income to the candidate? (2) When a secured campaign loan defaults and the candidate's property is sold at a trustee's sale, how should this be reported? (3) When a candidate lends money to his committee at 0%, is repaid using bank loan proceeds, then re-lends at a higher rate, does this constitute personal use of campaign funds? (4) After a fraudulently secured loan results in a judgment, must the judgment still be reported as an outstanding loan on the SEI?

CONCLUSION: (1) The only violation is the misreporting of the interest rate; the committee can repay both principal and interest to the lender, and these repayments are not income to the candidate reportable on the SEI. (2) When the property is sold at a trustee's sale, the candidate has made either a contribution or loan to the committee (reportable on Schedule A or B), must report the property disposal on the SEI property schedule, and must list the purchaser at the trustee's sale as an income source on the SEI. (3) There is no personal use issue when a candidate is repaid a 0% loan and re-lends at a higher rate to cover borrowing costs; the bank loan may need SEI reporting. (4) A judgment replacing a loan is an "outstanding debt" that continues to be reportable on the SEI even though technically it is no longer a loan.

embedding.summary: FPPC internal memorandum addressing four hypothetical scenarios involving the reporting of campaign loans on campaign statements and Statements of Economic Interest (SEI), covering topics including misreported interest rates, trustee's sale of secured property, restructuring of candidate loans, and reporting of loan judgments.
